book.title: The Complete Turtle Trader
book.subtitle: The Legend, the Lessons, the Results
book.author: Michael W. Covel
book.focus: Trend following via channel breakouts with ATR-based position sizing
book.historical_context: Documents Richard Dennis's 1983-1988 Turtle trading experiment where novices were trained to trade futures using systematic trend-following rules

strategies.name: Turtle System One (S1)
strategies.description: Shorter-term breakout system using 20-day (4-week) price breakouts for entry with a 10-day (2-week) breakout exit in the opposite direction. Includes a filter rule that skips signals if the previous breakout was a winner.

markets.notes: The Turtles traded approximately 30 liquid futures markets. Modern implementations can trade any liquid market including stocks, ETFs, forex, and crypto.

entry_rules.long_entry: Buy when price makes a new 20-day high (highest price of last 20 trading days)
entry_rules.short_entry: Sell short when price makes a new 20-day low (lowest price of last 20 trading days)
entry_rules.filter_rule: Skip the S1 breakout signal if the PREVIOUS S1 breakout was a winner. Even if the previous signal was not taken (theoretical winner), still skip. Exception: If the previous breakout resulted in a 2N loss, take the current breakout.
entry_rules.filter_direction: Direction of previous breakout is irrelevant for the filter. A losing short followed by a new long or short breakout can be taken.
entry_rules.failsafe: If a filtered S1 signal is missed and the market keeps trending, enter at the S2 (55-day) breakout level as a backup entry.

exit_rules.profit_exit: Exit long when price makes a new 10-day low (2-week breakout in opposite direction). Exit short when price makes a new 10-day high.
exit_rules.stop_loss: 2N hard stop (2 x ATR(20) below entry for longs, above entry for shorts). Whichever exit is hit first.
exit_rules.first_day_stop: On the first day of trading a new position, use a 0.5N stop instead of 2N.
exit_rules.priority: Take whichever exit signal is hit first - the 2N stop or the 10-day breakout exit.

position_sizing_formula.description: Risk a fixed 2% of account equity per trade (one 'unit')
position_sizing_formula.formula: Number of contracts = (Account Equity x Risk%) / (N x Dollar_Per_Point x Stop_Multiple)
position_sizing_formula.example: Account $100,000, Corn N=7 cents, Dollar per point=$50. 2N=$700. Contracts = $2,000/$700 = 2.67, rounded down to 2.

N_calculation.description: N is the 20-day exponential moving average of True Range (ATR)
N_calculation.true_range: Maximum of: (High - Low), |High - Previous Close|, |Low - Previous Close|

unit_limits.closely_correlated_markets: Maximum 6 units across closely correlated markets (e.g., Gold + Silver)
unit_limits.loosely_correlated_markets: Maximum 10 units across loosely correlated markets
unit_limits.single_direction: Maximum 12 units total in one direction (long or short)

drawdown_reduction.rule: For every 10% drawdown in account, reduce unit size by 20%
drawdown_reduction.example: At -11% drawdown, unit risk drops from 2.0% to 1.6%. At -22% drawdown, drops to 1.28%.
drawdown_reduction.restoration: Increase unit sizes back as account recovers

correlation_management.description: Avoid overloading correlated markets. 1 unit of Corn + 1 unit of Soybeans = effectively 2 units of either.
correlation_management.long_short_offset: Net unit risk = Larger side - (Smaller side / 2). Example: 4 long units + 3 short units = 4 - (3/2) = 2.5 units of risk.

pyramiding.description: Add units as position moves in your favor to maximize winning trades
pyramiding.add_interval: Add 1 unit for every 1N (one ATR) the market moves in your favor
pyramiding.stop_adjustment: When adding a new unit, move ALL stops to the new unit's 2N stop level. This locks in profit on earlier units.
pyramiding.example: Long entry at 100, N=5. Add at 105, 110, 115, 120 (max 5 units). After unit 2 at 105, all stops move to 105-10=95.
pyramiding.risk_profile: With 5 units pyramided, total risk to original equity is approximately 2.4% if all stops are hit simultaneously.

indicators.name: 20-Day Breakout (Donchian Channel)
indicators.usage: Entry signal - new 20-day high or low

indicators.name: 10-Day Breakout (Donchian Channel)
indicators.usage: Exit signal - 10-day breakout in opposite direction

indicators.name: ATR (Average True Range) / N
indicators.usage: Position sizing, stop placement, pyramiding intervals

strategies.notes: The filter rule (skip after winner) reduces whipsaws but risks missing the start of big trends. The S2 failsafe addresses this. Turtles typically allocated half their capital to S1 and half to S2.

strategies.name: Turtle System Two (S2)
strategies.description: Longer-term breakout system using 55-day (11-week) price breakouts for entry and 20-day (4-week) breakout exit in the opposite direction. No filter rule - every breakout is taken.

markets.specific_markets_traded: Same as S1 - all liquid futures markets
markets.notes: S2 serves as both standalone system and failsafe for filtered S1 signals

entry_rules.long_entry: Buy when price makes a new 55-day high (highest price of last 55 trading days)
entry_rules.short_entry: Sell short when price makes a new 55-day low (lowest price of last 55 trading days)
entry_rules.filter_rule: NONE - every S2 breakout signal is taken regardless of previous outcome
entry_rules.notes: S2 catches the big trends that S1's filter may miss. Also serves as the failsafe entry when S1 signals are skipped.

exit_rules.profit_exit: Exit long when price makes a new 20-day low. Exit short when price makes a new 20-day high.

risk_management.position_sizing: Identical to S1 - 2% risk per unit, N-based sizing
risk_management.pyramiding: Same rules as S1 - add every 1N, max 5 units

indicators.name: ATR (Average True Range) / N
indicators.usage: Position sizing, stop placement, pyramiding intervals

strategies.notes: S2 is the simpler, more robust system. No filter rule means more trades but also more false breakouts. Jerry Parker emphasized that core simple breakout/moving average systems are the real edge. Parker later used systems closer to S2 with a 52-week moving average as his core signal.

strategies.name: Turtle System Three (S3) - Flair Account
strategies.description: A discretionary allocation Dennis allowed each Turtle to trade however they wanted, with a strict $50,000 loss limit. Referred to as the 'dreaded flair account.'

markets.notes: Turtles could trade anything they wanted

entry_rules.loss_limit: Maximum $50,000 loss. Exceed $50,001 and you are out of the Turtle program.

risk_management.notes: Almost all Turtles abandoned S3 within 6 weeks. After experiencing the discipline of S1/S2, discretionary trading felt wrong.

strategies.notes: Included for completeness. Not a real codifiable strategy. Key insight: even trained systematic traders abandoned discretionary trading quickly, reinforcing the value of rules-based approaches.

strategies.name: Post-Turtle Parker Adaptation (Reduced Leverage)
strategies.description: Jerry Parker's adaptation of Turtle rules after leaving Dennis. Core change: dramatically reduced leverage to target 15-20% annual returns instead of 100%+. Same entry/exit logic, much smaller position sizes. This became the template for the $1B+ Chesapeake Capital.

markets.notes: Parker traded up to 65 markets across all sectors. 'Maybe we trend follow with Chinese porcelain. Maybe we trend follow with gold and silver, or stock futures, or whatever the client needs.'

entry_rules.description: Core Turtle breakout rules (primarily S2 style), with longer-term parameters
entry_rules.key_filter: Low volatility at entry is preferred - 'If the recent volatility is very low, not $5 in gold, but $2.50 in gold, then we're going to throw in a very large position.'
entry_rules.moving_average: 52-week moving average as core trend filter (from Mount Lucas Management Index)
entry_rules.notes: Parker emphasized keeping parameters longer-term and simple. 'The minute it takes too much thinking and too much fancy work, it is going to be a very bad situation.'

exit_rules.description: Standard Turtle exits but Parker was prone to 'lighten up or take a portion of the profits'
exit_rules.stop_loss: N-based stops, same philosophy as original Turtle rules

risk_management.position_sizing: Reduced from 2% to approximately 0.5-1% per unit to target ~15-20% annual returns
risk_management.leverage: Much lower than original Turtle levels
risk_management.notes: Parker: 'If you're going to raise a lot of money, people will be very happy with 15% or 20%.' He raised over $1 billion.

strategies.notes: This is the most commercially successful adaptation of the Turtle rules. Key insight: reducing leverage dramatically while keeping the same rules allowed Parker to attract institutional money and build a billion-dollar firm. Win rate ~40%, profit from ~10% of trades, rest breakeven to small losses.

strategies.name: Salem Abraham Trend Following
strategies.description: Salem Abraham learned trend-following concepts from Jerry Parker and later attended a 4-day Dennis/Eckhardt seminar. Built a 20-year track record from Canadian, Texas, starting with $100K family money. Uses systematic trend-following with strong risk management.

entry_rules.description: Breakout-based trend following (specific parameters not disclosed in book)
entry_rules.philosophy: Buy new highs, sell new lows. 'To buy crude oil at $55? That's a hard bet to do when it's never been to $56. Never in the history of the world has it been to $56 and you say, It hit $55 today, I'm buying it.'

risk_management.core_principle: 'Just because you have a Lamborghini, you don't have to go 160 miles an hour. I'm never going past 30 and I'm going to control risk.'

strategies.notes: Abraham is the best example of someone who learned the Turtle philosophy indirectly and built a successful firm. His higher expectation (2.13%/month vs Parker's 1.38%) suggests he maintained higher leverage. Key lesson: the exact rules matter less than the philosophy of trend following + risk management.

fundamental_principles: Trade price only - no fundamental analysis, no news, no opinions
Follow trends - buy strength, sell weakness ('buy high, sell higher')
Cut losses short, let profits run
The entry matters far less than the exit and position sizing
Losses are a cost of doing business - expect to lose on 60-70% of trades
Big trends pay for all the small losses - expect profits from ~10% of trades
Diversify across uncorrelated markets to catch trends wherever they occur
Use volatility (N/ATR) to equalize risk across all markets
Never try to predict the magnitude or duration of a trend
Missing a big trend can ruin your year - always be in the market
psychological_requirements: Accept losing 7 out of 10 trades as normal and expected
Do not second-guess the system during drawdowns
Trade like a robot - creative genius is counterproductive
Think of money as a score, not as purchasing power
Expect long periods of doing nothing - no trading when markets don't move
Be willing to buy at all-time highs and sell at all-time lows
Don't wait for retracements - if signal fires, enter immediately
Keep focus on the statistics, not individual trades
key_quotes: We found that liquidations are vastly more important than initiations. If you initiate purely randomly, you do surprisingly well with a good liquidation criterion. - William Eckhardt
Pure price systems are close enough to the North Pole that any departure tends to bring you farther south. - William Eckhardt
The average batter hits maybe .280 and the average system might be successful 35 percent of the time. - Richard Dennis
It is better to risk taking many small losses than to risk missing one large profit.
Good traders apply every ounce of intelligence they have into the creation of their systems, but then they're dumbbells in following them. - Richard Dennis
Trend following is like a democracy. Sometimes it doesn't look so good, but it's better than anything else out there. - Jerry Parker
Mean reversion works almost all of the time. Then it stops and you're kind of out of business. - Jerry Parker

during_experiment.total_profit_for_dennis: $150 million gross, $110 million net to Dennis
during_experiment.turtle_split: 10-15% of profits to Turtles, 85-90% to Dennis
during_experiment.monthly_volatility: Typical monthly swings of -30% to +90%. Most Turtles were down 30-50% before big trends hit.
during_experiment.notes: Curtis Faith reportedly made $31.5M but traded 10-20x more capital than other Turtles. His performance rate was not necessarily better - he just had more capital allocated.

Jerry Parker.key_to_success: First to reduce leverage for institutional clients. Strong business acumen. Built infrastructure.

Tom Shanks.style: Aggressive - maintained original high-leverage Turtle approach

William Eckhardt.aum: Part of $3B+ managed by Dennis associates

Salem Abraham.notes: Second-generation Turtle, learned from Parker. 20-year track record.

Curtis Faith.outcome: Retired at 23, lost money in software ventures, no sustained trading record post-Turtle
reasons: No proven track record after leaving Dennis
Lost most wealth in non-trading ventures

Russell Sands.outcome: Left program after one year, later sold Turtle rules as seminars
reasons: Chose to monetize Turtle fame through education rather than trading

Sam DeNardo.outcome: Cut from program for questioning allocations
reasons: Wrote letter to Dennis questioning allocation fairness
Dennis interpreted it as making excuses for losses

why_some_failed: Could not psychologically handle long losing streaks (60-70% of trades lose)
Broke the rules - especially position sizing and adding to losers
Reduced leverage too much (killed compounding) or not enough (blew up)
Failed to build a real business - relied solely on Turtle fame
Let ego and competition with other Turtles override discipline
Mean reversion temptation - switched to strategies that 'felt' better but had fatal flaws
Lack of entrepreneurial drive to survive on their own without Dennis's safety net
Could not handle the boredom of doing nothing when markets were flat

dennis_1986_memo.finding: Four Turtles (Shanks, Rabar, Keefer, Svoboda) discovered that combining S1 and S2 signals doubled the expected worst-case drawdown from 50% to 80%
dennis_1986_memo.cause: When both S1 and S2 generate entry signals simultaneously, position size doubles
dennis_1986_memo.action: All Turtles ordered to reduce position sizes by 50%
dennis_1986_memo.lesson: Even the system's creators can miscalculate risk. Always validate with backtesting.

dennis_1988_blowup.description: Dennis himself lost 55% in April 1988 trading his own account and Drexel Burnham funds
dennis_1988_blowup.cause: Dennis was overtrading S&P 500 futures at 10-15x the units his Turtles were allowed. His risk was ~100x greater than the Turtles.
dennis_1988_blowup.lesson: Even the creator of the system violated his own rules on position sizing. Discipline is everything.

adaptations_needed: Markets are more efficient - expect lower returns than 1980s
Need more markets for diversification (30-65+ markets)
Lower leverage is essential for institutional capital
Automated execution replaces manual phone orders
Can apply to stocks, ETFs, forex, crypto - not just futures
Consider shorter lookback periods for faster-moving modern markets
Transaction costs are much lower now - benefits trend followers
modern_practitioners: Man AHL, Winton Group, and other large CTAs use similar principles

modern_performance_expectations.notes: Returns are lower than the 1980s-90s but the strategy remains profitable across multiple decades of live trading by multiple practitioners.

extraction_metadata.confidence: HIGH - The Turtle rules are the most completely documented proprietary trading system ever published. Multiple sources corroborate the rules.
extraction_metadata.book_rating_for_strategy_extraction: 9/10 - One of the best books for extracting complete, codifiable trading systems
